name: Mercer County Emergency Communications Center
blurb: Serves as the Public Safety Answering and Dispatch Point for Police, Fire, EMS, and Emergency Management Services for all of Mercer County. The center processes emergency 911 and non-emergency calls in a prompt, courteous, and professional manner.
body: Mercer Communications Center serves as a Public Safety Answering and Dispatch Point for Police, Fire, EMS, and Emergency Management Services for Mercer County.

Mission: It is the mission of the Mercer County Emergency Communications Center to enhance the quality of life of every person in Mercer County, West Virginia, by processing emergency 911 and non-emergency calls for service in a prompt, courteous, professional manner; striving for efficiency; to help save lives, protect property, and to assist the public and public safety response personnel; making Mercer County a safer community in which to live, work, and visit.

name: Adult Probation
blurb: Adult Probation supervises adults placed on probation within the judicial circuit. Document forms and information are posted on the official county page.

name: Mercer County Airport
blurb: The Mercer County Airport is a general-aviation airport serving the greater Bluefield/Princeton region under the oversight of the County Commission. Document forms and information are posted on the official county page.

name: Mercer County Animal Shelter
blurb: An open-admission county animal shelter responsible for providing humane care for stray and relinquished animals in Mercer County, WV. The shelter accepts animals from Mercer County residents and adopts pets to homes outside the county, with all adopted pets spayed or neutered before placement.
body: Mercer County Animal Shelter is an open admission county animal shelter. We are responsible for providing humane care for stray and relinquished animals in Mercer County WV. We accept animals from Mercer County residents only. We are able to adopt pets to homes outside of Mercer County as all our pets are spayed/neutered before leaving the shelter. We are rescue friendly to 501(c)(3) registered rescues.

name: Town of Athens
blurb: Athens is a community in Mercer County, West Virginia, home to Concord University. Municipal documents and information are posted on the official county page.

name: City of Bluefield
blurb: Known as "Nature's Air-Conditioned City," Bluefield, West Virginia blends small-town charm with a rich history rooted in the railroad and coal industries, offering cool summers, outdoor adventures, and a vibrant community with Bluefield State College.
body: Established in the late 19th century, Bluefield's story is intertwined with the railroad and the region's coal industry. Its strategic location earned it the nickname "Little New York" as it bustled with commerce and industry. This era shaped the city's architectural landscape, leaving behind grand Victorian buildings and a beautifully preserved downtown.
Surrounded by the Appalachian Mountains, Bluefield is an outdoor haven offering hiking trails with breathtaking overlooks, cycling paths, and pristine fishing spots.

Bluefield is a thriving community with a warm spirit. It boasts a diverse business community and Bluefield State College, a historically black college. The arts and culture scene is vibrant, with venues like the Bluefield Arts Center and the Granada Theater hosting various events.

name: Town of Bramwell
blurb: A picturesque historical town nestled along the Bluestone National Scenic River in Mercer County, West Virginia, established in 1888 and once noted for having the greatest number of millionaires per capita in the United States.
body: Bramwell is a picturesque historical town nestled along the Bluestone National Scenic River in Mercer County, West Virginia. Named after the civil engineer J.H. Bramwell, this beautiful town began as a coal mining hub in the 1880s, officially being established in 1888 and gaining notoriety as once having the greatest number of millionaires per capita in the United States. Welcome to the Town of the Millionaires.

name: Mercer County Commission
blurb: The Mercer County Commission is the elected governing body of the county, responsible for fiscal affairs, county levies, property administration, probate, and appointing members to county boards and authorities. The commission holds at least four regular public sessions per year at the courthouse.
body: The Mercer County Commission is the governing body of the county. Within West Virginia, fifty-four counties elect three-member commissions while Jefferson County has a five-member commission. Commissioners serve in part-time positions.

The Constitution provides certain powers to the county commission, but these powers are limited to "the manner prescribed by law." This means that the county commissions' powers must be expressly conferred by the Constitution or by acts of the Legislature. With respect to the powers of a county commission, the West Virginia Supreme Court of Appeals has stated that "a county court (now county commission) possesses only such powers as are expressly conferred upon it by constitutional or statutory provisions, together with such powers as are reasonably and necessarily implied in the full and proper exercise of powers expressly conferred upon it."

The West Virginia Constitution, Section 11, Article 9, specifically grants the following powers and duties to county commissions: the custody, through their clerks, of all deeds and other papers presented for record in their counties, with responsibility for their preservation or disposal as may be prescribed by law; the administration of the internal police and fiscal affairs of their counties, with authority to lay county levies, under regulations as may be prescribed by law; and to serve as the judge of the election, qualification, and return of their own members, and of all county and district officers, subject to regulations as may be prescribed by law.

Other duties and responsibilities are specified in chapter 7 of the West Virginia Code, particularly in §7-1-3. These include: preparation and adoption of budget for all county offices except judicial; jurisdiction in all matters of probate; appointment of guardians for minor children and receipt of court settlements; ownership and maintenance of county property; serving as Board of Canvassers; laying and disbursing county levies based on assessed property values; sitting as Board of Review and Equalization in February to hear appeals on property values for assessment purposes; appointing Fiduciary Commissioners to oversee and settle certain estates as required; appointing members of certain county boards, authorities, and public service districts; adopting ordinances and orders in areas of jurisdiction as prescribed by law; and approving purchase orders and payment vouchers for elected county offices except judicial.

County commissions are required by Section 9, Article 9 of the Constitution to hold four regular sessions (meetings) each year. These meetings must be held at the courthouse. Special sessions may be held throughout the year if called by the president of the Commission with the concurrence of at least one other commissioner. Notice of all meetings must be given and meetings are public, as required by the open meetings law, West Virginia Code §6-9A-3. Executive sessions may be held as authorized by law. Two commissioners in attendance at a meeting establish a quorum. At the first session of each year, the commissioners choose one of their members to serve as president.

name: Corrections
blurb: Corrections covers coordination with the Southern Regional Jail and inmate services for Mercer County. Document forms and information are posted on the official county page.

name: Convention & Visitors Bureau
blurb: Promotes Mercer County, West Virginia as a year-round Appalachian destination featuring outdoor adventure, history, culture, arts, and local cuisine. Activities include hiking, fishing, golfing, festivals, theaters, and art galleries.
body: Mercer County, West Virginia offers a uniquely Appalachian blend of outdoor adventure, history, and culture, providing a mix of relaxation and adventure. Visitors can enjoy the beauty of the mountains, explore historical sites, and indulge in local cuisine.

Outdoor experiences include hiking, fishing, and golfing. The county also features events and festivals that showcase local heritage and community spirit. For those interested in arts and entertainment, Mercer County has theaters and art galleries.

name: Day Report Center
blurb: The Day Report Center provides community-based alternative sentencing and supervision programs. Document forms and information are posted on the official county page.

name: Mercer County Development Office
blurb: A one-stop resource for starting, expanding, or relocating a business in Mercer County, which has a rich history of manufacturing, technology, and tourism and direct access to 8 major markets via I-77.
body: Mercer County is home to a rich history of manufacturing, technology, and tourism. I-77 runs through the heart of the county and provides quick access to 8 major markets in the region.
Our office serves as a one-stop resource for starting, expanding, or relocating your business. Our team stands ready to help you invest and bring your business to Mercer County.

name: Education
blurb: Education links to Mercer County Schools and WVU Extension services. Document forms and information are posted on the official county page.

name: Mercer County Office of Emergency Management
blurb: The Mercer County Office of Emergency Management coordinates preparation for, response to, and recovery from natural and man-made disasters to protect lives, property, and the environment throughout the county.
body: Our mission is to protect lives, property, and the environment by coordinating efforts among various agencies and organizations to mitigate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from emergencies and disasters. We strive to build a resilient community that is prepared to face any challenge.

Emergency Planning: We develop comprehensive emergency plans to address a wide range of potential hazards, including floods, severe storms, wildfires, hazardous materials incidents, and public health emergencies.
Community Preparedness: We educate and empower citizens to prepare for emergencies through public awareness campaigns, training programs, and community outreach initiatives.

Emergency Response: In the event of a disaster, we coordinate the response efforts of various agencies, including law enforcement, fire services, emergency medical services, and volunteer organizations. We work to ensure that resources are deployed effectively and that those affected receive the assistance they need.

Disaster Recovery: We assist individuals and communities in recovering from disasters by coordinating resources, providing information about assistance programs, and facilitating long-term recovery efforts.
Hazard Mitigation: We work to identify and reduce potential hazards in our community through proactive measures such as flood plain management, building codes, and public education.

The Mercer County Office of Emergency Management works collaboratively with local, state, and federal agencies, as well as community organizations and volunteers, to build a safer and more resilient Mercer County. All citizens are encouraged to develop a family emergency plan, assemble a disaster supply kit, and stay informed about potential hazards in the area.

name: Mercer County Fair
blurb: The Mercer County Fair document page hosts fair-related documents and information; check the official county site for current fair schedules and forms.

name: Floodplain Management
blurb: Mercer County's Floodplain Management program is administered through designated floodplain managers serving each municipality, including Athens, Oakvale, Princeton, Bluefield, and Bramwell.
body: Athens Floodplain Manager: Tim Pike — 202 S. State St., Athens, WV 24712 — Phone: 304-384-3525
Oakvale Floodplain Manager: Deborah Rachel — PO Box 187, Oakvale, WV 24739 — Phone: (304) 898-2287
Princeton Floodplain Manager: Alex Ellison — 800 Bee Street, Princeton, WV 24740 — Phone: 304-887-7894
Bluefield Floodplain Manager: Kerry Stauffer — PO Box 4100, Bluefield, WV 24701 — Phone: (304) 327-2458
Bramwell Floodplain Manager: Louise Stoker — P.O. Box 338, Bramwell, WV 24715 — Phone: (304) 248-7114

name: Glenwood Recreational Park
blurb: Glenwood Recreational Park is a lakeside park on Rt. 20 between Princeton and Bluewell offering fishing, boating, hiking, mountain biking, miniature golf, disc golf, picnic shelters, a wedding gazebo, and access to the adjacent Mercer County 4-H Camp.
body: Centered around a beautiful lake in Mercer County, WV, Glenwood Recreational Park is located on Rt. 20 between Princeton and Bluewell. Activities include fishing, boating, hiking, mountain biking, miniature golf, disc golf, picnicking, geocaching, tennis, pickleball, basketball, and playgrounds. Visitors may launch canoes, kayaks, or John boats with trolling motors at one of 3 boat launches. Gasoline boat engines are not permitted.

Glenwood Park is also one of the most popular outdoor wedding venues in Mercer County. Picnic shelters and the wedding gazebo can be booked from April 15th through October 31st. Year-round rentals are available at the Mercer County 4-H Camp located adjacent to the park.

The Mercer County 4-H Camp, tucked away on the back side of Glenwood Park, was built entirely from logs sourced from the surrounding area beginning in 1940. Facilities include a dining hall with commercial kitchen that seats 150, an assembly hall with stage and kitchenette that seats 100, and 2 bunkhouses that sleep 75 each.

name: Hatfield-McCoy Trails
blurb: The Hatfield-McCoy Trails system encompasses over 1,000 miles of professionally managed off-highway vehicle trails in the mountains of southern West Virginia, open 365 days a year and suitable for all skill levels.
body: The Hatfield-McCoy Trails is made up of over 1,000 miles of trails located in the mountains of southern West Virginia. As one of the largest off-highway vehicle trail systems in the world, it is open 365 days a year and offers something for every skill level.

Trails range from the scenic mountain views of Pinnacle Creek to the tight and twisting trails of Bearwallow. Many trails connect to West Virginia's ATV-friendly towns, where visitors can grab a bite to eat and experience Southern hospitality.

The trail system has received coverage in publications such as Dirt Wheels, ATV Illustrated, ATV Sport, Dirt Rider, and ATV Magazine, as well as television features on Outdoor Channel programs including Fisher's ATV World and Dirt Trax TV.

name: Mercer County Health Department
blurb: The Mercer County Health Department aims to empower residents to live healthy lives by providing quality, cost-effective healthcare including primary care, preventive services, wellness screenings, and immunizations through its Blue Prince Family Health clinic.
body: The goal of the Mercer County Health Department is to empower the individuals of Mercer County to live healthy lives in healthy communities by providing quality healthcare in an efficient, cost-effective manner.
Full clinic services at Blue Prince Family Health encompass primary and preventative care, wellness screenings, breast and cervical cancer screenings, and immunizations and vaccines.

name: Juvenile Probation
blurb: The Mercer County Juvenile Probation document page hosts related documents and forms; check the official county site for current information.

name: Litter Control
blurb: The Mercer County Litter Control document page supports the Keep Mercer Clean campaign; check the official county site for current documents and information.

name: Town of Oakvale
blurb: The Town of Oakvale document page hosts municipal documents and information; check the official county site for current details.

name: City of Princeton
blurb: The City of Princeton, known as the "Heart of Mercer County," is the region's economic and cultural hub, featuring historic downtown architecture, locally owned shops, restaurants, and access to Appalachian Mountain recreational opportunities.
body: The City of Princeton, known as the "Heart of Mercer County," is nestled amidst the beautiful landscapes of West Virginia. With its rich history and vibrant culture, Princeton stands as a beacon of tradition and progress.

The City's downtown area features a blend of historic architecture and modern amenities. Visitors can explore locally-owned shops, dine in charming restaurants, and experience the genuine hospitality of the community.

Surrounded by the scenic beauty of the Appalachian Mountains, outdoor enthusiasts can enjoy a variety of recreational activities, from biking to exploring nearby parks. Princeton's commitment to green spaces supports a healthy, active lifestyle.

As the economic and cultural hub of the region, Princeton's diverse population contributes to a dynamic mix of traditions. The City's commitment to education and community engagement fosters a sense of unity throughout Mercer County.

name: Mercer County Recycling Drop-off Center
blurb: The Mercer County Recycling Drop-off Center, located at the Sanitary Landfill at 749 Frontage Road in Princeton, WV, accepts plastics, metals, aluminum, and paper products Monday through Saturday.
body: The Mercer County Recycling Drop-off Center is located at the Sanitary Landfill at 749 Frontage Road, Princeton, WV.
Hours of Operation: Monday–Friday 7 AM to 5 PM; Saturday 8 AM to 1 PM.

Accepted materials: #1 & #2 Plastics (please remove lids from plastic bottles); all ferrous metals, including cans; all types of aluminum, including cans; newspapers, cardboard, and mixed paper (all paper must be bagged and cardboard should be broken down). All materials must be separated.

contact.hours: Monday–Friday 7 AM to 5 PM; Saturday 8 AM to 1 PM

name: Sheriff's Office
blurb: The Mercer County Sheriff's Office document page hosts department documents and forms; see the Sheriff's detail page and the official county site for current information.

name: WVU Extension — Mercer County
blurb: The West Virginia University Extension office in Mercer County provides research-based programs in agriculture, 4-H youth development, food and nutrition, health and wellness, financial planning, and home gardening to all county residents.
body: The West Virginia University Extension works to provide solutions in your community through key programs and resources, including agriculture and natural resources; 4-H and youth development; food and nutrition; health and wellness; financial planning; and home gardening.

These research-based programs and services are available to all Mercer County residents thanks to the support of community members, local organizations, volunteers, and local partners including the Board of Education and County Commission.

name: Bids & Proposals
blurb: This section provides access to current bids and proposals being solicited by Mercer County, including RFPs, RFQs, and invitations to bid.
body: The Mercer County bids page provides access to current bids and proposals solicited by the county, including Requests for Proposals (RFPs), Requests for Qualifications (RFQs), and invitations to bid, and includes details on current and awarded projects for those seeking to do business with the county. No specific active bid listings were returned in the retrieved page content.

name: Mercer County E-911 Advisory Board
blurb: The Mercer County E-911 Advisory Board advises on the county's emergency 911 communications system and addressing. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Airport Authority
blurb: The Mercer County Airport Authority is the governing board for the county's airport operations and facilities. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Bluewell Public Service District Board
blurb: The Bluewell Public Service District Board governs the public service district serving the Bluewell area of Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Board of Health
blurb: The Mercer County Board of Health is the governing body overseeing public health policy and the county Health Department. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Bramwell Public Service District Board
blurb: The Bramwell Public Service District Board governs the public service district serving the Bramwell area of Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Building Commission
blurb: The Mercer County Building Commission holds and finances county-owned buildings and facilities on behalf of the Mercer County Commission. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Community Action of Southeastern WV (CASEWV)
blurb: CASEWV, Inc. is a community action agency chartered in 1964—the third such agency established in West Virginia—dedicated to empowering individuals and families in southeastern West Virginia to achieve self-sufficiency and improve their quality of life.
body: Community Action of South Eastern West Virginia, Inc. (CASEWV) has been serving the people of southeastern West Virginia since its charter in 1964. As the third such agency established in West Virginia, CASEWV is dedicated to empowering individuals and families to achieve self-sufficiency and improve their overall quality of life.

Driven by a volunteer Board of Directors representing a diverse cross-section of the community—elected officials, private sector leaders, and representation of the limited-income sector—CASEWV ensures that the voices of those it serves are heard and their needs are met. The organization envisions safe, healthy, inclusive communities to support individuals and families in reaching their full potential.

name: Civil Service Commission
blurb: The Civil Service Commission administers civil-service processes for eligible Mercer County employees. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: National Coal Heritage Highway Authority Board
blurb: The National Coal Heritage Highway Authority Board is the governing body for the National Coal Heritage Highway in the Mercer County region. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Convention and Visitors Bureau Board
blurb: The Mercer County Convention and Visitors Bureau is a non-profit destination marketing organization and the official tourism marketing body for Mercer County, funded by the six percent lodging tax paid by visitors.
body: The Mercer County Convention and Visitors Bureau is a non-profit destination marketing organization in Mercer County, West Virginia. Through digital and print marketing and social media, the Mercer County CVB promotes Mercer County and the municipalities of Athens, Bluefield, Bramwell and Princeton. The CVB is the official tourism marketing organization for the county and is funded by the six percent lodging tax paid by visitors to Mercer County, West Virginia.
Mission Statement: The Mercer County Convention and Visitors Bureau creates economic growth promoting the county as a destination.

name: Mercer County Development Authority
blurb: The Mercer County Development Authority promotes business investment in Mercer County, which has a history in manufacturing, technology, and tourism and is served by I-77 with access to eight major regional markets.
body: Mercer County is home to a rich history of manufacturing, technology, and tourism. I-77 runs through the heart of the county and provides quick access to 8 major markets in the region. The Mercer County Development Authority team stands ready to help businesses invest and locate in Mercer County.

name: Mercer County Dilapidated Structures Committee
blurb: The Mercer County Dilapidated Structures Committee reviews and prioritizes abatement of abandoned and deteriorating structures under the county's dilapidated buildings ordinance. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Fire Board
blurb: The Mercer County Fire Board coordinates and supports fire protection services and the county's volunteer fire departments. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Glenwood Park Board
blurb: The Glenwood Park Board oversees Glenwood Recreational Park facilities. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Green Valley-Glenwood Public Service District
blurb: Green Valley-Glenwood PSD, founded in 1963, provides quality and affordable water, sewer, and sprinkler services to residential and commercial customers in Mercer County.
body: At Green Valley-Glenwood PSD, our mission is to provide quality and affordable Water & Sewer services to our customers. We strive to continuously improve our services and to be environmentally responsible in our operations.
Since Green Valley-Glenwood PSD was founded in 1963, we have been dedicated to serving our community. We have grown and expanded over the years, but our commitment to our customers remains unchanged.

We offer a wide range of utility services, including water, sewer, and sprinkler services. Our services are available to both residential and commercial customers, and we are always working to provide new and innovative solutions.

name: Hatfield Trail
blurb: The Hatfield Trail board supports access to the Hatfield-McCoy Trail system in Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: King Coal Highway Authority
blurb: Established by the West Virginia Legislature in March 1999, the King Coal Highway Authority promotes and advances the construction of a modern highway through McDowell, Mercer, Mingo, Wyoming, and Wayne counties along US Route 52.
body: The King Coal Highway Authority was established by the West Virginia Legislature in March of 1999 to promote and advance the construction of a modern highway through McDowell, Mercer, Mingo, Wyoming and Wayne counties along currently existing US Route 52.

The Authority coordinates with counties, municipalities, state and federal agencies, public nonprofit corporations, private corporations, associations, partnerships and individuals for the purpose of planning, assisting and establishing recreational, tourism, industrial, economic and community development of the King Coal Highway for the benefit of West Virginians.
The Authority consists of 15 voting members and three ex-officio non-voting members.

name: Lashmeet Public Service District
blurb: The Lashmeet Public Service District provides water/sewer service in the Lashmeet area of Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mountain Resource Conservation District
blurb: The Mountain RCD works to maintain environmental quality and increase community development opportunities, assisting with identification, planning, and implementation of natural resource–related projects across twelve West Virginia counties, including Mercer.
body: The Mountain RCD exists to maintain environmental quality and increase community development opportunities. We assist with identification, planning, and implementing natural resource–related projects. Volunteer with the Mountain RCD to become more active in your community.
West Virginia Counties served: Braxton, Fayette, Greenbrier, McDowell, Mercer, Monroe, Nicholas, Pocahontas, Raleigh, Summers, Webster, and Wyoming.

name: Oakvale Public Service District
blurb: The Oakvale Public Service District provides water/sewer service in the Oakvale area of Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Mercer County Planning Commission
blurb: The Mercer County Planning Commission develops and implements comprehensive strategies to foster economic, cultural, and community growth throughout Mercer County, WV, guided by members uniquely qualified to assess local conditions and plan for the future.
body: The Mercer County Planning Commission is dedicated to improving the lives of the citizens of Mercer County, WV by creating, implementing, and maintaining a comprehensive strategy that will ultimately lead to economic, cultural, and community growth opportunities.

This Commission is comprised of individuals from throughout the County that are uniquely qualified to assess the local conditions, identify the capacity of opportunities, plan for those opportunities, implement strategies designed to change our current community climate, and evaluate our progress over time. In doing so, we will also be willing to work on sustaining these efforts over time, and doing so in a culturally acceptable fashion.

In building our comprehensive plan, we must identify land that is not intended for urban growth and set goals and programs for growth/decline management. We must identify plans and programs to promote the sense of community, character, and identity of Mercer County as a whole. We will identify specific resources for historical preservation, and mark preferred development areas, as well as renewal and/or redevelopment.
We are chartered with developing a financial program for goals and objectives that need public financing, and we will develop the action items needed to implement these strategies.

The Planning Commission will work to develop a timeline that will showcase how we meet our short and long term goals and objectives, and identify programs to encourage regional planning for the community as a whole.

name: Public Defender Board
blurb: The Public Defender Board supports indigent-defense services in Mercer County. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: Region I Planning & Development Council
blurb: Region I is a public agency and one of eleven Regional Planning & Development Councils in West Virginia, providing comprehensive planning and development services across six counties and twenty-seven municipalities in southern West Virginia.
body: Established as a public agency, Region I is one of eleven Regional Planning & Development Councils created by West Virginia Code, Chapter 8, Article 25, to provide comprehensive planning of all types for our service area, consisting of six counties and twenty-seven municipalities located within these counties. The mayors, county commissioners, and up to 49% private sector appointees comprise Region I's Board of Directors. We also work closely with local Economic Development Authorities, Public Service Districts, the Fayette Raleigh Metropolitan Planning Organization, New River Transit Authority, and various other local, state, and federal organizations throughout the region.

Region I utilizes federal, state, and local funding to assist with projects concerning infrastructure development, including expansion and replacement of water and sewer facilities, employment, industry, housing, health, recreation, and environmental protection. By coordinating closely with our affiliates in the region, we promote stability, growth, and progress in southern West Virginia.

Additionally, Region I offers professional Geographic Information System (GIS) and Global Positioning System (GPS) services to both governmental entities and private sector parties, including demographic analysis, utilities mapping, project area mapping, asset inventory, and custom-designed services.
WV Association of Regional Councils – Celebrating 50 Years of Service.

name: Mercer County Solid Waste Authority
blurb: The Mercer County Solid Waste Authority (MCSWA) is governed by five volunteer board members appointed to four-year terms under WV Code 22C-4-3, meeting monthly on the 4th Monday.
body: The Authority's Board of Directors is comprised of five volunteer members who are appointed to four-year terms in accordance with WV Code 22C-4-3: one by the Secretary of the D.E.P, two by the Mercer County Commission, one by the Mercer County Conservation District, and one by the P.S.C. Mr. Paul Canterbury is a charter member of the board, having served since its inception in January 1989. The MCSWA meets monthly on the 4th Monday (dates may vary if the regular date falls on a holiday). The members serve with no compensation.

contact.meets: Monthly on the 4th Monday (dates may vary if the regular date falls on a holiday)

name: Southern Regional Community Corrections
blurb: Southern Regional Community Corrections provides regional alternative-sentencing and reentry programming. Details are maintained on the official county site.

name: WV Workforce
blurb: WV Workforce serves Mercer County residents from its Princeton, WV office location at 195 Davis St., Suite 102.

name: Careers
blurb: This section lists career opportunities with Mercer County government, describing positions with competitive benefits and a supportive work environment.
body: The Mercer County careers page invites residents to explore career opportunities with the county, highlighting competitive benefits and a supportive work environment. No specific job listings or position titles were returned in the retrieved page content.

name: Documents
blurb: This section provides a directory of official Mercer County documents including levies, budgets, and comprehensive plans.
body: The Mercer County documents page provides a directory of county documents, including but not limited to levies, budgets, and comprehensive plans. No individual document links or items were returned in the retrieved page content.

name: Mercer County Assessor
blurb: The Mercer County Assessor's Office fairly determines the value of all real estate and personal property in Mercer County, West Virginia, which forms the basis for property taxes that fund essential county services.
body: The Mercer County Assessor's Office fairly determines the value of all real estate and personal property in Mercer County, West Virginia. This includes homes, commercial buildings, land, and vehicles. These assessments are the basis for property taxes, which fund essential county services.

Appraisers use established methods and technology to ensure accurate valuations, considering factors like location, size, condition, and market values. The office is committed to transparency and provides property owners with access to information about their assessments.
Services include information on property tax exemptions (Homestead, veterans, seniors), assistance understanding your assessment, and online access to property records and tax maps.

name: Mercer County Circuit Clerk
blurb: The Clerk of the Circuit Court is a full-time elective office that serves as the registrar, recorder, and custodian of all pleadings and documents in the Circuit and Family Courts, and administers the county's jury system.
body: The Clerk of the Circuit Court is a full-time elective office as authorized by Article 8, Section 9 of the West Virginia Constitution. Circuit Clerks are elected for a period of six years, and their duties, responsibilities, and compensation are set by statute.

The Circuit Clerk is an officer within the Judicial System and is the registrar, recorder, and custodian of all pleadings and documents in the Circuit and Family Courts. The Clerk is also responsible for the administration and management of the Jury System within the county.

Because the Clerk works with all the entities and agencies that comprise the justice system, communication and cooperation are essential. The Circuit Clerk's Office provides procedural assistance and information to pro se litigants.

name: Mercer County Circuit Court Judges
blurb: The Mercer County Circuit Court Judges preside over the Circuit Court, which handles felony criminal cases, civil matters, and appeals from lower courts.
body: This page provides access to relevant documents, forms, and reports related to the Mercer County Circuit Court when available.

name: Mercer County Commissioners
blurb: The Mercer County Commissioners are elected officials who oversee the government business of Mercer County, West Virginia.
body: The Mercer County Commission is the governing body of Mercer County. Commissioners are elected officials responsible for overseeing county government operations, budgeting, policy-making, and representing the county at state and national levels.

Commissioner Greg Puckett serves as the Region III representative. He was first elected in November 2014 and is currently serving his second term (January 1, 2021 - December 31, 2026). He is currently the Executive Director of Community Connections Inc. (CCI), the County's Family Resource Network.

Since being elected, Commissioner Puckett has worked with fellow Commissioners to re-establish the Mercer County Planning Commission, expand litter control efforts through the Keep Mercer Clean Campaign, establish tighter policies and ordinances to ensure accountability, and pay off the county debt to the regional jail.

Commissioner Puckett serves on the Board of Directors of the National Association of Counties (NACo), is Chair of NACo's Programs and Services Committee, Vice Chair of NACo's Healthy Counties Initiative, and has served on the National Opioid Taskforce and NACo's Economic Mobility Taskforce.

people.name: Greg Puckett
people.title: Commissioner, Region III (Second Term: January 1, 2021 - December 31, 2026)

name: Mercer County Clerk
blurb: The Mercer County Clerk is responsible by statute for the management, recordation, and preservation of official documents, election administration, vital records, voter registration, and a range of other county fiscal and administrative functions.
body: The Mercer County Clerk's responsibilities by statute include: the management, recordation, and preservation of official documents; election administration; fiscal officer duties including payroll, accounts payable, assistance in budgeting, accounting, and bookkeeping; all matters probate; issuance of vital records (birth, death, marriages); voter registration; hunting and fishing licenses; Civil Service Secretary; and Notary services.

Vital document request forms are available on the Documents tab of the office's website. Applications may be submitted in person at the Mercer County Clerk's Office, 1501 W. Main St., Suite 121, Princeton, WV 24740.

Photo ID is now required to vote in West Virginia. Residents needing a valid photo ID or assistance with voter registration may contact the Voter Registration Division. Absentee ballot applications for qualified voters (military, citizens abroad, voters with physical disabilities, and first responders on duty) are processed through the West Virginia Secretary of State's portal.

contact.address: 1501 W. Main St., Suite 121, Princeton, WV 24740

name: Mercer County Family Court Judges
blurb: Family court judges hear cases about the pressing issues facing Mercer County families, including divorce, abuse, neglect, family support, and paternity.
body: Family court judges hear cases about the pressing issues facing Mercer County families, including divorce, abuse, neglect, family support, paternity, and more.
This page provides access to relevant documents, forms, and reports related to the Mercer County Family Court when available.

name: Mercer County Magistrate Judges
blurb: Mercer County Magistrate Judges oversee the application and enforcement of state laws, municipal laws, and court procedures, including civil cases up to $10,000, misdemeanor cases, and preliminary examinations in felony cases.
body: There are 160 magistrates in West Virginia. There are at least two magistrates in every county, and ten in the largest county, Kanawha.

Magistrates use their training and sound judgment to oversee the application and enforcement of state laws, municipal laws, and court procedures. Magistrates have jurisdiction over civil cases in which the financial amount in dispute is not more than $10,000. They hear misdemeanor cases and conduct preliminary examinations in felony cases.

In criminal cases, magistrates issue and record affidavits, complaints, arrest warrants, and search warrants, as well as set bail and make decisions concerning proposed plea agreements, the collection of court costs, cash bonds, and fines.

Magistrates issue emergency protective orders in cases involving domestic violence. Immediately entering domestic violence petitions into the Domestic Violence Database and Registry is an important part of their work.

name: Mercer County Prosecuting Attorney
blurb: The Mercer County Prosecuting Attorney's Office prosecutes criminal cases on behalf of the State of West Virginia in Mercer County Circuit Court.
body: The Mercer County Prosecuting Attorney's Office handles criminal prosecution in Mercer County. Office staff include attorneys handling discovery, legal secretary functions, and felony matters in Circuit Court.

name: Mercer County Sheriff
blurb: The Mercer County Sheriff's Department is dedicated to safeguarding the community through a blend of time-honored values and modern law-enforcement strategies.
body: The Mercer County Sheriff's Department is dedicated to safeguarding the community through a blend of time-honored values and modern strategies.
This page provides access to relevant documents, forms, and reports related to the Mercer County Sheriff's Department when available.

name: Abandoned and Dilapidated Building Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance addresses abandoned and severely dilapidated buildings declared public nuisances and hazards to citizens of Mercer County, establishing an enforcement agency and procedures for abatement.
body: Adopted under authority of Chapter 7, Article 1, Section 3ff of the West Virginia Code, this ordinance applies to all properties in unincorporated Mercer County (excluding U.S. and WV state government properties and agricultural properties). It covers dwellings, mobile homes and recreational vehicles, certain other buildings, and refuse or toxic spillage on land containing a covered structure—all declared unreasonably unsafe, unsanitary, dangerous, or materially detrimental to public safety or welfare.

The ordinance creates an Enforcement Agency whose membership includes the County Engineer, County Health Officer, a Fire Chief from a county fire company, the County Litter Control Officer, three members at large appointed from the general public, and the Sheriff as an ex-officio member. Agency members serve two-year terms and meet monthly in public session; four members (two of whom must be members at large) constitute a quorum.

The County Commission serves as the hearing authority and may order repair, improvement, vacating, closing, removal, or demolition of covered structures; impose civil monetary penalties on non-compliant owners or possessors; enter into contracts to fulfill orders at county expense; and bring civil proceedings. The ordinance explicitly excludes agricultural improvements and materials and prohibits its use to implement zoning restrictions, building codes, or visual/beautification standards.

name: Exotic Entertainment Restriction Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance restricts the location of businesses offering exotic entertainment in Mercer County to at least 2,000 feet from churches, schools, parks, residential properties, family-oriented restaurants, licensed alcohol premises, and lodging establishments.
body: Enacted pursuant to West Virginia Code § 7-1-3ii, the ordinance defines 'exotic entertainment' as live entertainment, dancing, or other services conducted by persons while nude or semi-nude in a commercial setting or for profit, and 'semi-nude' is defined with specificity. No person, entity, or business may establish or operate an exotic entertainment business within 2,000 feet of a church, synagogue, mosque, or temple; any public or private educational facility (including day care and universities); a public park or recreation area; a residential property line; a restaurant or entertainment business oriented primarily toward children or families; a licensed alcoholic beverage premises; or a hotel, motel, campground, or bed and breakfast.

The ordinance also prohibits an exotic entertainment business from locating within 2,000 feet of another exotic entertainment business, or within the same building as another such business. Measurements are taken in a straight line from the nearest portion of the building used for exotic entertainment to the nearest property line of a protected use, without regard to intervening structures, objects, or political boundaries.

Enforcement is the responsibility of the County Commission, which may issue a written Violation Notice and Directive to Cease and Desist and, upon continued non-compliance, seek an injunction in Mercer County Circuit Court through the County Prosecuting Attorney. Any person adversely affected may seek direct judicial review. The ordinance expressly states it is not intended to suppress speech protected by the First Amendment but rather to address secondary effects of such businesses.

name: Fireworks Discharge Restriction Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance restricts the days, hours, and proximity within which consumer fireworks may be discharged in unincorporated Mercer County, and requires temporary retail vendors to register with the County Commission and pay a $100 fee.
body: Adopted pursuant to West Virginia Code § 7-1-3kk and effective June 1, 2019, the ordinance prohibits discharge of permitted fireworks except on specified dates and times: New Year's Eve between 9:00 p.m. and 11:59 p.m.; New Year's Day between 12:00 a.m. and 12:30 a.m.; June 24 through July 7 between 9:00 p.m. and 11:59 p.m.; and July 5 from 12:00 a.m. until 12:30 a.m. Discharge is never permitted within 500 feet of any residence, motel, hotel, bed and breakfast, hospital, nursing home, or other facility where people live, sleep, or rest.

The ordinance defines 'fireworks' to include consumer fireworks, display fireworks, and special effects, but expressly excludes sparkling devices, novelties, top caps, and bottle rockets. Display fireworks may only be used by professional pyrotechnicians licensed by the State Fire Marshal. Temporary independent retail vendors must register with the County Commission Office, obtain a copy of the ordinance, and pay a $100 fee before establishing a temporary location. Vendor operating hours are limited to 9:00 a.m. to 12:00 a.m. during permitted periods; Independence Day sales may run June 20 through July 7.

The Sheriff of Mercer County is authorized to regulate violations. Violations are misdemeanors punishable by fines of up to $500, with each individual discharge constituting a separate offense. Cases involving alleged violations are heard by the Mercer County Commission.

name: Mercer County Floodplain Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance establishes a floodplain area in Mercer County and requires permits for all development, construction, substantial improvement, or relocation of buildings or structures within that area, setting minimum construction standards and penalties for non-compliance.
body: The ordinance makes it unlawful for any contractor, person, partnership, business, or corporation to undertake development, new construction, substantial improvement, repair of substantial damage, or placement or relocation of any structure (including manufactured homes) within Mercer County without first obtaining a permit application and a certificate of compliance from the Floodplain Administrator. The Mercer County Commission serves as the Floodplain Administrator and may delegate this authority. Where land is to be subdivided or developed as a manufactured home park, a site plan must be submitted to and approved by the Floodplain Administrator prior to any development.

The identified floodplain area is defined as those areas subject to the one-hundred (100) year flood as shown on the Flood Insurance Rate Map (FIRM) and described in the Flood Insurance Study (FIS) prepared for Mercer County by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) dated March 2, 2005, or the most recent revision thereof. Key definitions include: Base Flood as the 100-year flood; Substantial Damage as damage where repair cost equals or exceeds 50% of the pre-damage market value; and Substantial Improvement as any repair, reconstruction, or addition costing 50% or more of the structure's pre-construction market value.

The ordinance promotes the general health, welfare, and safety of the community; encourages appropriate construction practices to prevent or minimize flood damage; protects water supply and sanitary sewage disposal; and reduces financial burdens on the community by preventing unwise development in flood-prone areas. Permits have a six-month validity period measured from the date of issuance.

name: Mercer County Litter Control Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance prohibits littering on public and private property in Mercer County, establishes duties for property owners and businesses to maintain litter-free premises, and sets penalties ranging from $50 to $500 with the option of community service.
body: Enacted December 9, 2008, under authority of West Virginia Code § 7-1-3kk, the ordinance defines 'litter' broadly to include garbage, refuse, trash, disposable packages and containers, bottles, glass, cans, scrap metal, abandoned vehicles, appliances, furniture, paper, plastic, rubber, cigarette or cigar butts, animal carcasses, and construction site waste materials, but excludes wastes of primary processes of mining, logging, sawmilling, farming, or manufacturing. It is unlawful for any person to drop, deposit, discard, or otherwise dispose of litter on any public or private property in Mercer County except in authorized receptacles or designated disposal sites. Operators of vehicles from which any load escapes must immediately clean the public property and bear any costs; violation is a misdemeanor with a fine of $50 to $1,000 or 8–16 hours of community service.

The ordinance assigns specific duties: commercial establishments and institutions must keep adjacent and surrounding areas clear of litter; residential property owners and tenants must keep exterior private property free of litter; junk or abandoned vehicles must be kept inside an enclosed building or screened from public view; and owners, agents, or contractors at construction or demolition sites must provide litter receptacles, collect litter daily, and remove all litter at least once per week. Any indication of ownership found in litter constitutes prima facie evidence of violation.

Stationary violation penalties are $50–$500; a court may also order community service to remove litter. Second and subsequent offenses are subject to double the penalty range. Ten days after notice, the Mercer County Administrator is authorized to remove litter from private property and charge the costs to the owner. Prosecution may be initiated by any law enforcement officer or private citizen. The ordinance was enacted and ordained December 9, 2008, by Commission President Joe Coburn and Commissioners Karen Disibbio and John Anderson.

name: Mental Hygiene – Involuntary Hospitalization (WV Code § 27-5-1 et seq.)
blurb: The page reproduces West Virginia State Code Chapter 27, Article 5, governing involuntary hospitalization proceedings, including the appointment and duties of mental hygiene commissioners, prosecuting attorneys, and sheriffs in Mercer County mental hygiene matters.
body: The page publishes West Virginia Code § 27-5-1 (effective June 2020) and related sections, which govern the process of involuntary hospitalization of individuals believed to be mentally ill or to have a substance use disorder and likely to cause serious harm to themselves or others. The chief judge in each judicial circuit appoints competent attorneys as mental hygiene commissioners to preside over involuntary hospitalization hearings; commissioners must complete orientation training provided by the Supreme Court of Appeals and the Department of Health and Human Resources before presiding over hearings.

Mental hygiene commissioners may issue summonses and subpoenas, place witnesses under oath, elicit testimony, and make findings of fact and conclusions of law (which are not binding on the circuit court). The prosecuting attorney or an assistant represents applicants in final commitment proceedings. Upon written order of the circuit court, mental hygiene commissioner, or magistrate, the sheriff must take the individual into custody, transport them to and from the hearing and the mental health facility, and maintain custody during the hearing.

Under § 27-5-2, any adult may apply for involuntary custody for examination of an individual when there is reason to believe the individual is mentally ill or has a substance use disorder and is likely to cause serious harm. A probable cause hearing must generally be held immediately (or within 24 hours under a standing court program), and the individual has the right to be present, present evidence, confront witnesses, remain silent, and be represented by appointed counsel. A diagnosis of dementia alone may not serve as a basis for involuntary commitment.
adopted: Effective June 5, 2020 (§ 27-5-1, Acts 2020, c. 273); current with legislation of the 2020 Regular Session

name: Mercer County Canine Control Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance prohibits dog owners in unincorporated Mercer County from allowing their dogs to run at large, authorizes Animal Control Officers and law enforcement to seize and impound dogs found at large, and establishes fines of $25–$100 per violation.
body: Enacted under West Virginia Code § 19-20-6 and amended May 6, 2025 (entered June 24, 2025), the ordinance applies to all dogs in the unincorporated areas of Mercer County. Owners must confine dogs on their premises in a humane manner consistent with WV Code § 61-8-19, which requires proper sustenance (food, water, shelter from the elements) and prohibits cruel chaining or tethering. No dog may be taken onto streets, sidewalks, or public places unless on a leash or otherwise under physical control. Exceptions are made for 'farm dogs' protecting livestock and 'hunting dogs' engaged in lawful hunting while equipped with an active tracking device being followed by the owner.

A dog is deemed 'at large' whenever it is not under the immediate physical control of a responsible person capable of restraining it and is not on the owner's property or property where it has express permission to be. Animal Control Officers, Dog Wardens, and law enforcement officers are required to seize any dog found at large—whether licensed or unlicensed—and impound it consistent with WV Code §§ 19-20-6 and 19-20-8. Cruel tethering is specifically defined and prohibited, including use of restraints exceeding one-eighth the animal's body weight, restraints that cause choking, or restraints under approximately 15 feet in length that prevent the animal from urinating or defecating in a separate area.

An owner may redeem an impounded dog within five days of notice by paying all assessed costs and any fine, after which the owner must obtain a license from the courthouse and provide a current rabies vaccination per WV Code § 19-20A-2. Violation is a misdemeanor; fines range from $25 to $100 (in addition to court costs). Each incident of a dog being at large in any 12-hour period constitutes a single violation.

name: Mercer County Spay Neuter Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance requires residents to spay or neuter all dogs and cats over six months of age kept in Mercer County, with exceptions for licensed breeders and specific medical or situational exemptions, and establishes an Unaltered Animal License (UAL) system enforced by Animal Control.
body: Dated June 10, 2025, the ordinance makes it unlawful to own, possess, or keep any dog or cat over six months of age in Mercer County unless the animal has been spayed or neutered, or the owner holds a valid Unaltered Animal License (UAL) issued annually by the Mercer County Commission. Exceptions include: animals certified in writing by a locally licensed veterinarian (within 100 miles) as medically unfit for sterilization or too young by breed standard; animals present in the county fewer than 30 days per year (e.g., tourism, foster care, part-time residency); service animals for residents with documented disabilities (but not Emotional Support Animals); and animals harbored by a recognized humane society or advocacy group (exempt for 90 days from intake).

The UAL is issued annually (expiring June 30 each year), is nontransferable, and costs $25 for groups of 1–5, 6–10, 11–15, or 16–20 unaltered animals per year, or a one-time lifetime fee of $50 per animal for owners with three or fewer intact animals who meet four criteria: valid Mercer County Animal Tax Tag, current rabies vaccination certificate from a licensed veterinarian, a photo of the animal, and a current microchip certificate showing the animal's name and implant number. No UAL may be issued to any person convicted of crimes against animals. UAL holders who advertise animals for sale must prominently display their permit number in all advertisements. Selling, trading, or giving away any unaltered animal without a UAL is a violation.

Penalties for violations are: first offense $150 (or $125 with paid microchip); second offense $300 or 30 hours community service; third offense $500 or 50 hours community service; fourth offense $1,000 per offense; fifth and subsequent offenses include fines plus jail time as determined by the court. Fines and fees collected under the ordinance (up to $10,000 per fiscal year) are deposited into a Spay Neuter Assistance Fund to provide financial assistance to qualified Mercer County residents for spay and neuter of companion animals. An animal impounded three times within two years will be microchipped at the owner's expense before redemption; a fourth impoundment triggers mandatory sterilization at the owner's expense before redemption or adoption.

name: Mercer County Noise Ordinance
blurb: This ordinance prohibits unnecessary, excessive, and annoying noise disturbances in Mercer County, specifying restrictions on amplified sound, yelling, and animal noise, and setting penalties escalating from $5–$50 for a first offense to $100–$500 or up to 90 days in jail for a third or subsequent offense.
body: Enacted September 13, 2005, the ordinance recognizes that noise at certain levels is detrimental to the health, welfare, safety, and quality of life of county inhabitants and declares it county policy to reduce noise and prohibit unnecessary, excessive, and annoying noise from all sources subject to its police power. It is unlawful for any person to knowingly keep or harbor any animal that habitually barks, howls, cries, or yelps to the great discomfort of the neighborhood, with such animals declared a public nuisance. Complaints trigger a warning from the Sheriff's Department; if ineffective, a verified complaint signed by at least two citizens (not from the same household) or a deputy sheriff may be filed with a magistrate, and the owner cited.

A 'noise disturbance' is defined as any sound that by its character, intensity, and duration endangers or injures health or safety, or annoys or disturbs persons of normal sensitivities. Specific prohibitions include: operating amplified audio equipment, televisions, musical instruments, or similar devices between 12:00 a.m. and 7:00 a.m. in a manner audible across a residential property boundary, or operating such devices in or on a motor vehicle on a public right-of-way at a volume audible more than 50 feet away; and yelling, shouting, whistling, or singing between 12:00 a.m. and 7:00 a.m. on a public right-of-way, or at any time across a residential property boundary or in a manner disturbing occupants of any office, hotel, or motel.

Exemptions include sounds necessary for protection of property or life; police, fire, and emergency vehicle sirens and horns; parades, fireworks, school-related activities and other special events; activities on county and school athletic facilities authorized by the property owner; fire and burglar alarms (with notice and reasonable opportunity to shut off); religious services including music, bells, chimes, and organs; locomotives and aircraft; the striking of clocks; and any noise from activities permitted by law or county or state license. As an additional remedy, noise disturbances may be abated by restraining order or injunction. Penalties: first offense $5–$50; second offense $25–$100; third or subsequent offense $100–$500 and/or up to 90 days in county jail.

name: Mercer County Ordinances
blurb: The Mercer County ordinances index provides access to the regulatory orders and ordinances adopted by the Mercer County Commission.
body: The page serves as a directory for Mercer County ordinances. The content returned by the page describes it as providing a comprehensive directory of Mercer County offices, including contact information, locations, and operating hours, but no further ordinance-specific index content was present in the retrieved text.

name: Parks
blurb: This section provides a directory of WV State Parks, County Parks, and City Parks located in and around Mercer County, with addresses, trail information, park websites, and family activity details.
body: The Mercer County parks page provides a comprehensive directory of West Virginia State Parks, County Parks, and City Parks located around Mercer County. The page includes addresses, information about trails, park websites, and family activities. No individual park names, addresses, or links were returned in the retrieved page content.
